hypotheses: Hypothesis 1 (Replication of van Dijk et al., 1999). Keeping the expected return constant, outcome variance will not influence an individual's contribution toward a public good as long as the distribution of possible outcomes includes gains-only prospects.

Hypothesis 2. Keeping the expected return constant, outcome variance will decrease an individual's contributions toward a public good only when the distribution of possible outcomes includes the prospect of losses.

Hypothesis 3a. Outcome distributions including the prospect of losses-and-gains (compared to outcome distributions including only the prospect of gains) will increase the likelihood of loss aversion as a determinant of contribution behavior.

Hypothesis 3b. Outcome distributions including the prospect of losses-and-gains (compared to outcome distributions including only the prospect of gains) will increase the likelihood of fear of others' non-contribution as a determinant of contribution behavior.
Hypothesis 4 (Replication of Franzen, 1995). An individual's likelihood of contributing toward the public good will be less when group size is large than when group size is small.

Hypothesis 5. Group size will moderate the relationship between outcome variance and the likelihood of cooperation such that outcome distributions that contain both losses-and-gains (compared to outcome distributions that contain gains-only) will more negatively influence the rate of cooperation when group size is small than when group size is large.
claim.hypotheses_location: Hypotheses 1–3a/3b are stated in the text preceding Methods – Study 1 under the heading 'This leads to the following hypotheses:' (end of the introduction/beginning of Study 1). Hypotheses 4 and 5 are stated in the Study 2 section before Methods – Study 2 (under 'Social uncertainty in assurance public good dilemmas' and 'Interaction of social uncertainty and outcome prospects').
claim.statement: Contributions were negatively affected by loss prospects in the distribution of possible outcomes.
claim.statement_location: Abstract (summary sentence: 'Contributions were negatively affected by loss prospects in the distribution of possible outcomes.') and reiterated in the General Discussion/conclusion.

data.source: Laboratory experiments with undergraduate participants (two within-subjects experimental studies).
data.wave_or_subset: Study 1: 12 independent games per participant (3 threshold levels × 4 outcome variance conditions). Study 2: 6 assurance dilemmas per participant (3 outcome variance conditions × 2 group sizes).
data.sample_size: Study 1: initial N=72, final N=62 after exclusions. Study 2: initial N=84, final N=65 after exclusions.
data.unit_of_analysis: Individual participant decisions (contribution amount per game in Study 1; binary cooperation decision per game in Study 2).
data.notes: Within-subjects designs. Study 1: each participant made 12 independent contribution decisions; Study 2: each participant made 6 dichotomous contribution decisions. Outcome distributions were uniform over specified bounds; mean expected values were held constant across conditions. Some participants were removed for incomplete data or misunderstanding the games; removals did not change hypothesis test outcomes according to authors. No dataset links or code links provided in the paper.

method.description: Two laboratory experiments with undergraduate participants examined how outcome variance in public good provision (distinguished as gains-only vs losses-and-gains prospects) affects contribution behavior. Study 1 used a step-level public goods paradigm with continuous contributions (participants had 125 points to allocate to shared or personal accounts) across 12 games varying provision threshold (low/medium/high) and outcome variance (no, low, medium, high). Study 2 used assurance public goods dilemmas with all-or-nothing contributions (125 points) across 6 games varying outcome variance (no variance, gains-only, losses-and-gains) and group size (2 vs 8). Behavioral measures included contribution amount (Study 1) or cooperation (Study 2), strategy-choice self-reports (reasons for contributing or not), and a single-item fear-of-incurring-loss measure. Manipulation checks assessed perceived certainty of portion of bonus and identification of threshold.
steps: Design within-subjects experimental conditions manipulating outcome variance (uniform distributions with fixed mean) and either provision threshold (Study 1) or group size (Study 2).
Recruit undergraduate participants and bring them individually to the lab.
Provide written instructions and require passing a comprehension quiz; coach participants until correct if necessary.
Present each game scenario in randomized/blocked order (Study 1: blocked by threshold with counterbalanced orderings; Study 2: Latin square ordering).
Collect contribution decisions for each independent game (Study 1: continuous allocation of 125 points to shared/personal accounts; Study 2: dichotomous contribution of 125 points or none).
After each decision, collect strategy-choice (single selection from provided reasons) and fear-of-incurring-loss rating and manipulation-check items.
Randomly combine participant decisions into groups (including across sessions) to determine payoffs; select one decision to pay real money at 1 point = $0.01.
Analyze data using repeated-measures ANOVA and chi-square tests (Study 1) and Generalized Estimating Equations/logistic analyses (Study 2); report means/proportions and relevant test statistics.
method.models: Study 1: repeated-measures ANOVA for continuous contribution amounts; chi-square tests for categorical strategy-choice comparisons. Study 2: Generalized Estimating Equations (GEE) producing logistic-type coefficients tested with Wald chi-square statistics for binary cooperation outcome; chi-square tests for strategy-choice comparisons. Manipulation checks via ANOVA.
method.outcome_variable: Study 1: Contribution amount (number of points out of 125 allocated to shared account per game). Study 2: Cooperation indicator (binary: contributed 125 points to shared account = 1; defect = 0).
method.independent_variables: Outcome variance condition (no variance, gains-only, losses-and-gains realized via varying uniform distribution bounds), outcome prospects (gains-only vs losses-and-gains determined by combination of threshold and distribution), provision threshold (Study 1: low=160, medium=300, high=440 points), group size (Study 2: 2 vs 8 participants).
method.control_variables: Threshold level controlled/blocked in Study 1 analyses; analyses accounted for within-subject design. No additional covariates (e.g., demographic controls) reported.

results.summary: Across two laboratory experiments, outcome variance reduced contributions only when the outcome distribution included the prospect of losses. When distributions entailed gains-only prospects, outcome variance did not significantly affect contributions. Strategy-choice self-reports and a fear-of-incurring-loss item indicated that loss aversion and increased fears that others would not contribute mediated or accompanied decreased contributions under losses-and-gains prospects. Group size (social uncertainty) reduced cooperation (lower cooperation in larger groups), but did not significantly moderate the negative effect of loss prospects on cooperation.

numerical_results.p_value: reference comparison: no significant difference vs gains-only condition (F1,61 < 1.00, p > .95)
see comparison with no variance: p > .95
F1,61 = 4.73, p < .05 (participants contributed significantly less in losses-and-gains vs no variance/gains-only)

numerical_results.outcome_name: Study 1 - proportion selecting non-contribution strategies (losses-and-gains vs others)
Study 1 - strategy: loss aversion chosen (losses-and-gains vs others)
numerical_results.direction: positive (more loss aversion selections in losses-and-gains)

numerical_results.outcome_name: Study 1 - strategy: fear of others' non-contribution (losses-and-gains vs others)

numerical_results.p_value: no significant difference vs gains-only: Wald v2(1) = 0.10, p > .74
Wald v2(1) = 19.74, p < .001 (less cooperation in losses-and-gains vs no variance/gains-only)

numerical_results.outcome_name: Study 2 - group size effect on cooperation
numerical_results.value: higher cooperation in small groups than large groups
numerical_results.direction: negative (larger group size associated with lower cooperation)

numerical_results.outcome_name: Study 2 - interaction between outcome variance and group size

numerical_results.value: 18% in losses-and-gains vs 0% in no variance/gains-only

numerical_results.outcome_name: Fear of incurring a loss (combined findings)
numerical_results.value: higher fear ratings in losses-and-gains than in no variance/gains-only

metadata.original_paper_title: Is it even worth it? The effect of loss prospects in the outcome distribution of a public goods dilemma
